---
title: "Baselines"
subtitle: "What classical methods achieve on Norwegian macro data"
---

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
metrics = FileAttachment("_data/combined_metrics.json").json()
```

## Method overview

We evaluate five classical baselines on the validation era (2006-2015, 120 monthly rolling forecast origins):

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **Random Walk** | Forecast = last observed value. The simplest possible baseline. |
| **Seasonal Naive** | Forecast = same month from previous year. |
| **AR(p)** | Autoregressive model with BIC lag selection, direct h-step forecast. |
| **ARIMA** | Auto-selected ARIMA(p,d,q) with AIC-optimal order per origin. |
| **ETS** | Exponential smoothing with automatic trend/seasonal selection. |

Plus the **Chronos-2** foundation model (zero-shot, no covariates, no fine-tuning).


## Performance comparison

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof selectedMetric = Inputs.radio(
  ["rmse", "mae", "mase"],
  {label: "Metric", value: "rmse"}
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: fig-comparison
//| fig-cap: "Performance across methods and horizons. Lower is better."

{
  const metricKey = `avg_${selectedMetric}`;
  const data = metrics.methods.flatMap(m =>
    Object.entries(m.summary).map(([h, s]) => ({
      method: m.display_name,
      horizon: +h,
      value: s[metricKey],
      category: m.category
    }))
  );

  const categoryColors = {
    "naive": "#95a5a6",
    "classical": "#2ecc71",
    "foundation": "#9b59b6"
  };

  return Plot.plot({
    height: 400,
    marginLeft: 60,
    marginBottom: 45,
    x: {
      label: "Forecast horizon (months)",
      ticks: [1, 3, 6, 12]
    },
    y: {
      label: selectedMetric.toUpperCase(),
      grid: true
    },
    color: {
      domain: metrics.methods.map(m => m.display_name),
      range: metrics.methods.map(m => ({
        "naive": "#bdc3c7",
        "classical": "#27ae60",
        "foundation": "#8e44ad"
      })[m.category] || "#999")
    },
    marks: [
      Plot.line(data, {
        x: "horizon", y: "value", stroke: "method",
        strokeWidth: 2, curve: "monotone-x"
      }),
      Plot.dot(data, {
        x: "horizon", y: "value", fill: "method",
        r: 4, tip: true
      })
    ]
  });
}
```


## Detailed results by variable

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof selectedVariable = Inputs.select(
  ["cpi", "industrial_production", "retail_sales", "unemployment"],
  {label: "Target variable", value: "cpi", format: v => v.replace(/_/g, " ").replace(/\b\w/g, c => c.toUpperCase())}
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: fig-variable-detail
//| fig-cap: "Method comparison for the selected variable."

{
  const mk = selectedMetric;
  const data = metrics.methods.flatMap(m => {
    const varMetrics = m.metrics[selectedVariable] || {};
    return Object.entries(varMetrics).map(([h, vals]) => ({
      method: m.display_name,
      horizon: +h,
      value: vals[mk],
      category: m.category
    }));
  });

  return Plot.plot({
    height: 380,
    marginLeft: 60,
    marginBottom: 45,
    x: {
      label: "Forecast horizon (months)",
      ticks: [1, 3, 6, 12]
    },
    y: {
      label: mk.toUpperCase(),
      grid: true
    },
    marks: [
      Plot.line(data, {
        x: "horizon", y: "value", stroke: "method",
        strokeWidth: 2, curve: "monotone-x"
      }),
      Plot.dot(data, {
        x: "horizon", y: "value", fill: "method",
        r: 4, tip: true
      })
    ]
  });
}
```


## Relative to random walk

How much does each method improve over the naive random walk baseline? A ratio below 1.0 means the method is better.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: fig-relative
//| fig-cap: "RMSE relative to Random Walk. Values below 1.0 (dashed line) indicate improvement."

{
  const rw = metrics.methods.find(m => m.name === "random_walk");
  const others = metrics.methods.filter(m => m.name !== "random_walk");

  const data = others.flatMap(m =>
    Object.entries(m.summary).map(([h, s]) => {
      const rwVal = rw.summary[h]?.avg_rmse || 1;
      return {
        method: m.display_name,
        horizon: +h,
        ratio: s.avg_rmse / rwVal
      };
    })
  );

  return Plot.plot({
    height: 380,
    marginLeft: 60,
    marginBottom: 45,
    x: {
      label: "Forecast horizon (months)",
      ticks: [1, 3, 6, 12]
    },
    y: {
      label: "RMSE ratio (vs Random Walk)",
      grid: true,
      domain: [0.8, 1.3]
    },
    marks: [
      Plot.ruleY([1.0], {stroke: "#999", strokeDasharray: "6,4", strokeWidth: 1.5}),
      Plot.line(data, {
        x: "horizon", y: "ratio", stroke: "method",
        strokeWidth: 2, curve: "monotone-x"
      }),
      Plot.dot(data, {
        x: "horizon", y: "ratio", fill: "method",
        r: 4, tip: true
      }),
      Plot.text([{x: 12, y: 1.0, text: "Random Walk"}], {
        x: "x", y: "y", text: "text", dy: -10, fontSize: 9, fill: "#999"
      })
    ]
  });
}
```


## Key takeaways

1. **ARIMA is the best classical baseline** — consistently lowest average RMSE across horizons
2. **Random walk is surprisingly hard to beat** at long horizons (h=12), where AR and ETS actually perform worse
3. **Zero-shot Chronos-2 does not beat ARIMA** — it's competitive at h=1 but falls behind at longer horizons
4. **The gap is the opportunity** — this is exactly what the agentic search aims to close through covariate selection, transformations, and fine-tuning
